params.description: Three bags appear on screen, each containing 4 items.

beats.text: In our Warmup, you saw that graph scales are equal groups. But equal groups show up everywhere, not just in graphs. Today you're going to learn the name for these groups, see them in all kinds of pictures, and discover a new math symbol that makes them easy to write down. Check this out.

elements.description: Three bags on screen, each containing 4 items.

beats.text: Let me show you how I figure out the groups here. First, I count the bags.

params.description: Bags highlight sequentially as they are counted: 1, 2, 3.

beats.text: First, count the groups. How many bags? 1, 2, 3. Three bags. These are my groups.

elements.description: Three bags on screen, all highlighted. Each contains 4 items.

params.description: First bag zooms in or highlights. Items inside are counted: 1, 2, 3, 4.

beats.text: Next, look inside one bag. How many? 1, 2, 3, 4. Four items in each bag. That's how many are in each group.

elements.description: Three bags on screen. First bag highlighted, showing 4 items counted inside.

beats.text: So I have three groups of four. Say it with me: three groups of four.

elements.description: Three bags on screen, each containing 4 items.

params.description: Equation 3 × 4 appears below the bags.

beats.text: We can write three groups of four like this: 3 × 4.

elements.description: Three bags on screen, each containing 4 items.

incorrects.description: Student selected 1 (counted items instead of containers)

params.description: Boxes themselves highlight to distinguish them from items inside.

steps.text: Count the BOXES, not what's inside them. How many boxes do you see?

incorrects.description: Student selected 3 (miscounted containers)

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially: 1, 2.

steps.text: Let's count together. Watch the boxes light up: 1, 2. How many boxes?

incorrects.description: Student selected 5 (counted items per container instead of containers)

params.description: Box outlines highlight to distinguish containers from contents.

steps.text: You're counting what's INSIDE. First, count just the boxes. The containers themselves.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially: 1, 2. System demonstrates counting.

steps.text: Here, let me show you. I count the boxes, not what's inside. 1, 2. Two boxes. Those are the groups. When we count groups, we count the containers first. The answer is 2.

incorrects.description: Student selected 2 (counted groups instead of items per group)

params.description: First box zooms or highlights. Items inside become focal.

steps.text: Now look INSIDE one box. Count what you see in that one box, not the boxes themselves.

incorrects.description: Student selected 3 (miscounted items in container)

params.description: Items in one box highlight sequentially: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

steps.text: Let's count the items in one box together: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. How many in each box?

incorrects.description: Student selected 10 (counted all items across all groups)

params.description: First box zooms or highlights to focus on just one group.

steps.text: You counted everything. Look at just ONE box. How many items are in that single box?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: One box highlights. Items inside are counted sequentially: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

steps.text: Let me walk you through it. I pick one box and count what's inside: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Each box has 5 items. That's what we mean by 'in each group.' The answer is 5.

incorrects.description: Student selected 5 groups of 2 (reversed groups and items per group)

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially: 1, 2.

steps.text: You switched them. First number is the GROUPS. How many boxes? Then look inside ONE box. How many in each?

incorrects.description: Student selected 2 groups of 10 (correct group count, but counted all items as items per group)

params.description: One box zooms. Items inside that box highlight.

steps.text: You got the groups right: 2 boxes. But now count just ONE box. Don't count both boxes together.

incorrects.description: Student selected 10 groups of 2 (counted total items as groups)

params.description: Box outlines highlight to distinguish containers from items.

steps.text: Groups are the CONTAINERS. Count the boxes first, not the items. How many boxes?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially (1, 2), then one box zooms and items inside count (1, 2, 3, 4, 5).

steps.text: Let me show you the whole process. First, I count the boxes: 1, 2. Two groups. Then I look inside one box and count: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five in each. So I have 2 groups of 5. That's how we name equal groups: groups first, then how many in each. The answer is 2 groups of 5.

incorrects.description: Student set wrong container count on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Listen to what I asked for: 3 groups of 4. How many groups?

incorrects.description: Student set wrong container count on second attempt

params.description: The phrase '3 groups' highlights in the instruction area.

steps.text: Look at the instruction: 3 groups of 4. The first number tells you how many bags. How many bags do you need?

incorrects.description: Student set wrong container count three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: 3 bags appear on screen, demonstrated by the system.

steps.text: Let me show you. The problem says 3 groups of 4. Groups means bags. The first number, 3, tells me how many bags. So I need 3 bags. That's what groups means in multiplication.

incorrects.description: Student set wrong items per container on first attempt

steps.text: Almost. The instruction says 3 groups of 4. How many in each group?

incorrects.description: Student set wrong items per container on second attempt

params.description: The phrase 'of 4' highlights in the instruction area.

steps.text: Look closely: 3 groups of 4. The second number tells you how many items go in each bag. How many items in each?

incorrects.description: Student set wrong items per container three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: 4 items appear in each of the 3 bags, demonstrated by the system.

steps.text: Here's how it works. The instruction says 3 groups of 4. The second number, 4, tells me how many items go in each bag. So I put 4 items in each of the 3 bags. That builds 3 groups of 4. The number after 'of' tells you what's in each group.

incorrects.description: Student set wrong container count on first attempt

steps.text: Count the groups I asked for. How many groups?

incorrects.description: Student set wrong container count on second attempt

params.description: The number 4 pulses or highlights in the prompt area.

steps.text: I said 4 groups. That means 4 bags. Set the number to 4.

incorrects.description: Student set wrong container count three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: 4 bags appear on screen, demonstrating the correct container count.

steps.text: Let me show you. 4 groups means 4 bags. I set the number to 4, and now 4 bags appear. The first number tells us how many groups.

incorrects.description: Student set wrong items per container on first attempt

steps.text: Check the number. How many in each group?

incorrects.description: Student set wrong items per container on second attempt

params.description: The number 3 pulses or highlights in the prompt area.

steps.text: I said 3 in each group. That means 3 items go into every bag. Set the number to 3.

incorrects.description: Student set wrong items per container three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: 3 items appear in each of the 4 bags, demonstrating the correct items per container.

steps.text: Here's how it works. 3 in each means every bag gets 3 items. I set the number to 3, and now all 4 bags have 3 items each. That's 4 groups of 3, which we write as 4 × 3.

incorrects.description: Student selected 5 groups of 2 — reversed the groups and items per group

params.description: The 2 boxes highlight to emphasize the number of groups.

steps.text: You're counting what's inside. Count the boxes first. That's your groups. How many boxes do you see?

incorrects.description: Student selected 2 groups of 2 — counted groups correctly but undercounted items per group

params.description: Items in the first box highlight.

steps.text: You got the groups right. Now look inside one box. Count all the items carefully. How many?

params.description: The 2 boxes highlight.

steps.text: Let's count the boxes. Just the boxes. How many boxes are there?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially: 1, 2. Then items in one box highlight sequentially: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

steps.text: Here's how to think about it. First, count the boxes: 1, 2. Two groups. Then look inside one box and count: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five in each group. So we have 2 groups of 5. That's what equal groups means — same number in each one.

incorrects.description: Student selected 5 × 2 — reversed the groups and items in the expression

params.description: The 2 boxes highlight.

steps.text: The first number is the groups. Count the boxes. How many boxes? That number goes first.

incorrects.description: Student selected 2 + 5 — used addition instead of multiplication

params.description: Equation area highlights or the × symbol appears emphasized.

steps.text: Addition joins numbers. Multiplication is for equal groups. We have groups with the same amount in each. That's what × means.

incorrects.description: Student selected 5 + 2 — used addition and reversed the order

params.description: Equation area highlights or the × symbol appears emphasized.

steps.text: We're not adding. We're counting equal groups. The × symbol shows groups. What are the groups here?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Boxes highlight to show 2 groups, then one box's items highlight to show 5 items. Expression 2 × 5 appears or highlights.

steps.text: Let me show you. We have 2 boxes — that's our groups. Each box has 5 items — that's in each group. When we write it with ×, the first number is the groups: 2. The second number is in each group: 5. So it's 2 × 5. Multiplication shows equal groups.

incorrects.description: Student selected 2, confusing items per container with container count

steps.text: You counted what's inside. Count the circles themselves. Each circle is a group.

steps.text: Don't count the dots yet. Just the circles. How many circles do you see?

steps.text: Let me show you. Count the circles with me: 1, 2, 3, 4. Four circles means four groups.

steps.text: Let's count together. Circles: 1, 2, 3, 4. Four groups. Now one circle: 1, 2 dots. Two in each. So you have 4 groups of 2.

steps.text: You have equal groups here, not adding two numbers. When every group has the same amount, use ×. Which symbol shows groups?

params.description: All circles highlight together to show they are equal groups.

steps.text: This shows equal groups, not adding. The × symbol shows you have groups with the same amount in each. Look for ×.

params.description: Circles highlight sequentially: 1, 2, 3, 4. Then one circle zooms, dots count: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

steps.text: Here's how to think about it. Count the circles: 4. That's your first number. Count inside one circle: 5. That's your second number. The × shows equal groups. The answer is 4 × 5.

steps.text: That's adding. Equal groups need multiplication. The × symbol means groups of the same size. Look at the expressions again. Which one uses ×?

params.description: System first highlights all five circles sequentially (1, 2, 3, 4, 5), then zooms to one circle and counts the three dots inside (1, 2, 3).

steps.text: Let me show you. First, count the circles: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five groups. Now look inside one circle: 1, 2, 3. Three dots in each group. That gives us 5 × 3. The first number is always the groups. The second number is what's in each group. The answer is 5 × 3.

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially: 1, 2. Then one box zooms to show 4 items inside, counted 1, 2, 3, 4.

steps.text: Let me show you. First, I count the boxes. 1, 2. Two boxes. Those are the groups. Now I look inside one box: 1, 2, 3, 4. Four items in each group. So it's 2 groups of 4, which we write as 2 × 4.
